one_line_takeaway: The first systematic study showing that KG-RAG systems are highly vulnerable to stealthy, black-box, insertion-only KG poisoning — as few as ~20 crafted triples per question can flip most correct answers (e.g., RoG EM 45.76%->8.85% on WebQSP), with the retrieval stage the primary point of failure.
key_finding: Even minimal, stealthy KG perturbation substantially degrades all four KG-RAG methods, while the random baseline causes only marginal drops. The dominant vulnerability is the retrieval stage: at least one adversarial triple is retrieved for over 90% of attacked questions (RoG A-RR reaches 99.63% on CWQ, 95.44% on WebQSP). The generation stage is more resilient but 'polarized' — LLMs either fully reject the adversarial content or, once adopted, let it dominate the prediction. A notable trend: LLMs with stronger KG-reasoning ability are MORE sensitive to poisoning — the KG-specific LLMRoG has the best clean F1 but the largest relative F1 degradation (45.89%). Attack effectiveness varies by method (RoG most vulnerable; GCR most robust, attributed to its two-LLM design that separates candidate generation from final answer selection) and by dataset (WebQSP more susceptible than the harder CWQ). Increasing k beyond 4 yields diminishing returns while raising detection risk.
core_contribution: The paper presents what the authors claim is the first systematic study of data poisoning attacks against knowledge-graph-based RAG (KG-RAG) systems. Prior poisoning work targeted unstructured-text RAG (PoisonedRAG, BadRAG) or KG-embedding models; this paper's own contribution is a black-box, insertion-only attack that pollutes the underlying KG rather than a text corpus. The attack (1) prompts a general-purpose LLM (GPT-4) to produce plausible-but-wrong 'adversarial answer' entities and fuzzy-matches them to existing KG entities, (2) uses a KG-specific LLM (LLMRoG) to generate question-relevant relation paths as templates, and (3) grounds each path prefix from the question topic entity and attaches the adversarial answer as the final tail, inserting perturbation triples that complete misleading multi-hop inference chains. A fallback samples random bridge entities when the KG is too sparse to ground a path.
threat_model: Explicitly formalized (Section 2.3). Attacker objective: inject a small set of triples into the external KG so KG-RAG returns answers that exclude the gold answer. Knowledge: fully black-box — attacker can inspect/modify the KG (entities, relations, topology) but has NO access to the retriever, LLM architecture/parameters, or prompt templates, and does NOT know ground-truth answers. Following prior text-RAG attack work, the attacker may reuse an LLM to build the attack without violating the black-box assumption, since it never observes the deployed system's internals or outputs. Capabilities/constraints: insertion-only (no deletion or modification of existing facts, reflecting Wikidata-style edit permissions); every inserted triple must reuse existing entities and relations (no new nodes/edges, for stealth); at most K perturbation triples per adversarial answer, with K << |triples|.
limitations: By the authors' own framing: (1) insertion-only — deletion and modification attacks are left to future work; (2) evaluated on only two KGQA benchmarks, both built on Freebase, so generalization to other KGs (Wikidata, medical KGs mentioned in motivation) is untested; (3) G-retriever is excluded from all CWQ results due to >100-hour processing time, leaving that cell of the comparison incomplete; (4) no defense or mitigation is proposed or evaluated — the paper only characterizes the vulnerability; (5) attack effectiveness is bounded by the LLM's implicit content-filtering (adversarial answers that are retrieved are often dropped at generation), which the authors flag as needing better understanding; (6) relies on availability of a KG-specific LLM (LLMRoG) for path generation, and GPT-4 for answer generation, so attack quality depends on these external models.

datasets_used: WebQSP (WebQuestionSP): 1,628 test questions, 65.49% 1-hop / 34.51% 2-hop, avg 4,228 triples per question subgraph, built on Freebase
CWQ (Complex WebQuestions): 3,531 test questions, 40.91% 1-hop / 38.64% 2-hop / 20.75% >=3-hop, avg 4,195 triples per question subgraph, built on Freebase
quantitative_results: RoG on WebQSP under attack: F1 70.34%->38.06% (46% relative drop), Hits@1 79.61%->48.46% (39% drop), EM 45.76%->8.85% (81% drop)
RoG on CWQ under attack: F1 54.86%->36.45% (34% drop), EM 47.18%->26.71% (43% drop)
SubgraphRAG on WebQSP: F1 66.94%->50.22% (25% drop), EM 51.29%->27.52% (46% drop)
GCR on WebQSP: F1 71.07%->63.94% (10% drop) — most robust path-retrieval method
G-retriever on WebQSP: F1 52.39%->46.31% (12% drop) — appears robust but has low clean performance (no CWQ results, >100h runtime)
Random-modification baseline (also 20 triples/question) causes only marginal drops, e.g. RoG WebQSP F1 70.34%->67.38%
Retrieval-stage coverage (A-RR): RoG 99.63% (CWQ), 95.44% (WebQSP); SubgraphRAG 87.84% (CWQ), 87.09% (WebQSP) — over 90% of questions have >=1 adversarial triple retrieved
Adversarial manipulation (Table 4) RoG A-Precision 49.66% (WebQSP) / 46.10% (CWQ), A-MRR 56.34% / 48.12%; GCR far lower (A-Precision 13.52% / 18.41%)

Generator-LLM robustness on RoG (Table 5): KG-specific LLMRoG best clean F1 70.34% but worst relative F1 degradation 45.89%; DeepSeek-V3-0324 highest post-attack EM 11.81% (surpassing LLMRoG's 8.85%); LLaMA-2-7B-chat-hf weakest, EM nearly eliminated (7.49%->0.25%)
Attack budget: N=5 adversarial answers x K=4 = up to 20 injected triples per question, far fewer than the ~4,200 average subgraph triples; increasing k beyond 4 gives diminishing returns
baselines_compared: Structure-preserving random modification (Rand): identifies triples involving the question entity and randomly replaces the other entity, capped at 20 corrupted triples per question — the only prior-art-free baseline since no existing KG-RAG poisoning attack existed
Clean (no-attack) scenario as the reference point for all degradation measurements
